text: What do main memory and registers have in common from the CPU's perspective?
info: The CPU can only access data directly from main memory and registers. It cannot directly access data from other storage devices.

answers.text: They are permanent storage that retains data after power-off
They are the only storage that the CPU can access directly
They are the slowest storage in the system
They have the largest capacity in the system

text: What is the purpose of the base and limit register pair?
info: The base register defines the starting address of a process's address space, and the limit register defines its size. The CPU checks every memory access to ensure it does not exceed the limit, preventing unauthorized access.

answers.text: Used to speed up memory access
Used to define a process's address space and prevent out-of-bounds access
Used to swap data between memory and disk
Used to manage the page table

text: How does execution-time address binding differ from compile-time and load-time binding?
info: Execution-time binding delays the mapping of logical addresses to physical addresses until runtime, allowing a process to be moved during execution. It requires hardware support such as a relocation register.

answers.text: The address is assigned at compile time and cannot be changed
The address is assigned at load time and remains fixed
The address is mapped at runtime, allowing the process to move in memory
The address is assigned by the user at runtime

text: What is the difference between a logical address and a physical address?
info: A logical address (virtual address) is generated by the CPU, while a physical address is the actual address in memory seen by the memory unit. They differ when execution-time binding is used.

answers.text: Logical address is generated by the CPU; physical address is the actual address in memory
Logical address is the actual address in memory; physical address is generated by the CPU
Logical address is used only in kernel space

text: What is the role of the relocation register in a memory management system?
info: The relocation register (or base register) holds the starting physical address of a process. This value is added to every logical address to produce the physical address, allowing a process to be placed anywhere in memory.

answers.text: Stores the size of a process to prevent overflow
Stores the starting physical address to be added to the logical address
Stores the page table base address

text: What is the advantage of dynamic linking over static linking?
info: Dynamic linking defers library linking until execution time using stubs, allowing libraries to be shared among processes, reducing executable size, and enabling easy library updates without recompiling programs.

answers.text: Libraries can be shared among processes and updated easily
No library is needed in the system
The program becomes larger in size

text: What is swapping in an operating system?
info: Swapping is the process of moving an entire process out of main memory to a backing store (disk) temporarily, then bringing it back to continue execution. This allows the system to run processes whose combined size exceeds physical memory.

answers.text: Swapping data between cache and main memory
Moving a process out of memory to disk and back

text: What is the main limitation of swapping in modern systems?
info: Swapping has high overhead because transfer time depends on process size, causes problems with pending I/O, and is not suitable for flash memory devices with limited write cycles.

answers.text: Cannot be done on multicore systems
Takes a long time depending on process size and causes I/O problems

text: In what context are the first-fit, best-fit, and worst-fit algorithms used?
info: These algorithms are used in contiguous memory allocation to select the appropriate hole (free space) when allocating memory to a process.

answers.text: Process ordering in the ready queue

text: Which allocation algorithms perform best in terms of speed and storage utilization based on analysis?
info: First-fit and best-fit outperform worst-fit in terms of speed and space usage. First-fit is generally fastest, and best-fit produces the least internal fragmentation.

text: What is external fragmentation?
info: External fragmentation occurs when there is enough total free space for a process, but it is scattered in non-contiguous pieces, making it impossible to allocate to a large process.

answers.text: Unused space inside an allocated partition
Sufficient total free space that is not contiguous
A page table that is too large

text: What is internal fragmentation?
info: Internal fragmentation occurs when the memory allocated to a process is larger than what the process actually needs. The excess space is inside the partition but unused.

answers.text: Unused space inside an allocated partition
Space wasted by the page table

text: What problem does compaction solve, and what condition is required?
info: Compaction solves external fragmentation by rearranging memory contents so that all free space is consolidated into one large block. It requires hardware support for dynamic relocation (execution-time binding).

answers.text: Solves internal fragmentation; requires static relocation
Solves external fragmentation; requires dynamic relocation

text: How does segmentation manage memory from the user's perspective?
info: Segmentation views memory as a collection of segments that are logical units, such as the main program, functions, stack, and arrays. Each segment has a different size and does not need to be contiguous in physical memory.

answers.text: Views memory as a set of fixed-size blocks
Views memory as a collection of logical units of varying sizes
Views memory as a contiguous area
Views memory as a set of equal-sized pages

text: In a segmentation architecture, what does a logical address consist of?
info: A logical address in segmentation consists of a segment number and an offset. The segment number is used as an index into the segment table to find the base address and limit of that segment.

text: How does paging solve the problem of external fragmentation?
info: Paging divides physical memory into fixed-size frames and logical memory into pages of the same size, allowing pages of a process to be placed in any available frames without needing to be contiguous, preventing external fragmentation.

answers.text: Uses fixed-size frames so no contiguous space is needed

text: How many bits does the page offset occupy in a logical address if the page size is 4 KB (2^12)?
info: The page offset size is log2(page size). With a page size of 2^12 bytes, the offset is 12 bits.

text: How many memory accesses are required in a paging system without a TLB?
info: Without a TLB, two memory accesses are needed: first to read the page table (to find the frame number), and second to read/write the actual data.

text: What is the function of the Translation Look-aside Buffer (TLB)?
info: The TLB is a small associative memory that caches recently used page table entries to reduce the number of memory accesses needed for address translation, using parallel search.

answers.text: Caches page table entries to speed up address translation
Stores the contents of frequently used pages

text: What is the benefit of ASID (Address-Space Identifier) in the TLB?
info: ASID identifies which process a TLB entry belongs to, eliminating the need to flush the entire TLB on every context switch and improving performance.

answers.text: Increases the size of the TLB
Identifies the owning process of each TLB entry, preventing full TLB flush on context switch

text: What is the valid-invalid bit in a page table used for?
info: The valid-invalid bit indicates whether a page belongs to the process's logical address space. It helps detect illegal memory accesses and prevents unauthorized access.

answers.text: Indicates whether the page is currently in memory
Indicates whether the page is in the logical address space and is a legal page
Indicates whether the page is shared

text: How does paging enable code sharing between processes?
info: Paging allows multiple processes to share the same physical memory pages by mapping each process's page table to the same frame. This works well with reentrant (read-only) code.

answers.text: Copies the code to every process
Maps multiple processes' page tables to the same frame
Stores the code in the TLB

text: Why was hierarchical page table (two-level paging) introduced?
info: Two-level paging reduces the size of the page table that must reside in memory by dividing it into multiple sections and paging the page table itself. It is suitable for large address spaces.

answers.text: To reduce the size of the page table that must stay in memory
To increase the number of frames

text: In two-level paging on a 32-bit machine with a page size of 4 KB, how is the logical address divided?
info: Page size 4 KB (2^12) → 12 bits for offset. The remaining 20 bits form the page number, split into p1 (outer page) and p2 (inner page).

answers.text: p1 (10 bits), p2 (10 bits), offset (12 bits)
p1 (12 bits), p2 (8 bits), offset (12 bits)
p1 (8 bits), p2 (12 bits), offset (12 bits)

text: What is the problem with using multi-level paging on a 64-bit address space?
info: A 64-bit address space greatly increases the number of page table levels required (potentially 4–5 levels), also increasing the number of memory accesses needed for address translation.

answers.text: No problem because hardware supports it
Number of page table levels and memory accesses both increase significantly

text: For which architecture is a hashed page table most suitable?
info: Hashed page tables are suitable for address spaces larger than 32 bits. They use a hash function to look up page table entries, reducing the size of the page table.

text: How does an inverted page table differ from a conventional page table?
info: An inverted page table has one entry per physical frame (not per logical page) and stores which process and virtual page the frame is mapped from. It is suitable for large address spaces.

answers.text: Has one entry per logical page
Has one entry per physical frame

text: When is a clustered page table most appropriate?
info: A clustered page table is a form of hashed page table where each entry refers to multiple pages (e.g., 16 pages). It is best suited for sparse address spaces where memory references are scattered non-contiguously.

text: Why is swapping unsuitable for flash memory devices in mobile devices?
info: Flash memory has a limited number of write cycles and low throughput between flash and CPU. Frequent swapping would reduce device lifespan and performance.

answers.text: Flash memory has limited write cycles and low throughput
Mobile operating systems do not support it

text: How does iOS handle low memory conditions?
info: iOS asks applications to voluntarily relinquish memory, especially read-only data that can be reloaded from flash. If an app does not comply, it may be terminated.

answers.text: Asks apps to voluntarily release memory

text: How does Android handle low memory conditions?
info: Android terminates applications when memory is low, but before termination it saves the application state so that it can be restarted quickly.

answers.text: Terminates apps and saves state for quick restart
Asks apps to voluntarily release memory

text: What is the disadvantage of a small page size?
info: A small page size reduces internal fragmentation but increases the size of the page table because more entries are needed to map the same address space.

text: Which statement is correct about address binding in real-time systems?
info: Execution-time binding requires hardware support (a relocation register). Each access must add the relocation register value, introducing a small overhead.

answers.text: Compile-time binding is suitable for systems that need to move processes
Execution-time binding requires no hardware support
Execution-time binding requires a relocation register and has slight overhead
Load-time binding can only be done with static linking

text: Given a page size of 2048 bytes and a process size of 72766 bytes, how much internal fragmentation results?
info: 72766 / 2048 = 35.53, so 36 pages are needed (36 × 2048 = 73728). Internal fragmentation = 73728 − 72766 = 962 bytes.

text: Why is standard swapping not used in modern operating systems?
info: Standard swapping has high overhead because transfer time depends on process size, and it causes problems with pending I/O. Modern systems use paging and a modified form of swapping only when memory is critically low.

text: In a system using a TLB with a hit rate of 98%, TLB access time of 1 ns, and memory access time of 100 ns, what is the effective memory access time?
info: Effective access time = (hit rate × (TLB time + memory time)) + (miss rate × (TLB time + 2 × memory time)) = 0.98 × (1+100) + 0.02 × (1+200) = 98.98 + 4.02 = 103 ns.

text: Why may the best-fit allocation algorithm not be the best in practice?
info: Best-fit requires searching the entire list of holes and tends to leave very small leftover holes (tiny holes) that cannot be used, resulting in high external fragmentation.

answers.text: It creates very small leftover holes

text: In a segmentation architecture, what does a segment table entry contain?
info: A segment table entry contains the base (starting physical address), limit (length of segment), protection bits, and a valid bit.

text: What problem occurs when swapping a process that has pending I/O?
info: If a process with ongoing I/O is swapped out, when the I/O completes, data would be written to the original memory address, which may already be used by another process, causing data corruption.

answers.text: Data may be overwritten by another process

text: What does double buffering in swapping mean?
info: Double buffering transfers I/O to a kernel buffer first, then copies it to user space. This prevents problems when a process is swapped out during I/O, but adds overhead.

answers.text: Uses two buffers to increase speed
Transfers I/O to a kernel buffer first to prevent swapping problems
Stores the buffer in both memory and disk
Uses separate buffers for read and write

text: In a paging system, will sequential memory access result in a high or low TLB hit rate?
info: Sequential access has high locality (spatial locality), resulting in a high TLB hit rate because the same page or the next page is likely already in the TLB.

text: What is a disadvantage of the inverted page table?
info: The inverted page table makes memory sharing difficult because each physical frame has only one entry. Special handling is needed to map multiple virtual addresses to the same frame.

answers.text: Shared memory is difficult to implement

text: What is the characteristic of reentrant code?
info: Reentrant code can be shared among multiple processes simultaneously because it never modifies itself (read-only), allowing a single copy to exist in memory.

answers.text: Read-only code that can be shared

text: In a paging system, what is the effect of increasing the page size?
info: A larger page size increases internal fragmentation but reduces the page table size. The TLB can also cover a larger portion of memory.

answers.text: Page table becomes smaller; internal fragmentation increases

text: Which statement about external fragmentation is correct?
info: External fragmentation arises from scattered non-contiguous holes that prevent large process allocation even when total free space is sufficient.

answers.text: Occurs in contiguous allocation and segmentation
Can be solved by using a larger page size
Does not exist in modern systems

text: What does the segment-table length register (STLR) check?
info: STLR stores the number of segments used by the program. It is used to verify that a requested segment number is valid (s < STLR), preventing access to non-existent segments.

answers.text: The number of segments used by the program
The base address of the segment table

text: What is the advantage of contiguous memory allocation?
info: Contiguous allocation is simple to implement and has low overhead, but suffers from external fragmentation.

text: Why do most modern operating systems prefer paging over swapping?
info: Paging does not require physical memory to be contiguous, reduces external fragmentation, and can load a process one page at a time, making memory use more efficient than swapping.

answers.text: Paging reduces fragmentation and can load one page at a time

text: In a paging system with a TLB but without ASID, what happens during a context switch?
info: Without ASID, the entire TLB must be flushed on every context switch to prevent a process from accessing another process's memory, reducing performance.

answers.text: The TLB must be flushed on every context switch

text: In a system where code is shared between processes using paging, which statement is correct?
info: Shared pages must be read-only to prevent modifications that would affect other processes. Each process has a page table that points to the same frame.

answers.text: Each process has a separate copy

text: What is the advantage of two-level paging over single-level paging?
info: Two-level paging allows parts of the page table to be paged out to disk when not in use, reducing memory usage for programs with large address spaces that use little of it.

answers.text: Parts of the page table can reside on disk
